region_of_origin: China (Sichuan / Hubei crayfish cuisine tradition); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Freshwater crustacean dish — crayfish (procambarus clarkii) cooked in seasoned golden broth with garlic

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the specific prepared dish 'Garlic Crayfish in Golden Broth'. FOODON_03311737 (crayfish food product) is the closest parent class covering crayfish-based food products.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers freshwater crayfish as an ingredient category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for the prepared dish with garlic and golden broth seasoning. This is the closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227261009 identifies crayfish as a substance. No SNOMED CT concept exists for the specific prepared dish. Used as closest parent anchor for the primary protein ingredient.

main_ingredients: golden broth (chicken or pork bone stock with turmeric or egg yolk base)

serving.description: 1 shared portion (approximately 500 g whole crayfish with broth)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Garlic Crayfish in Golden Broth is approximately 500 g of whole crayfish with broth, intended as a shared dish for 2–3 people; edible yield is approximately 35–40% of whole weight.

nutrient_source.title: Crayfish, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174202); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods garlic and broth components for composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for this specific prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked crayfish (FDC ID 174202) as the primary protein base, with adjustments for garlic, golden broth (chicken stock base), cooking oil, and seasoning additions typical of this dish style. Energy, protein, and fat values reflect the composite edible portion per 100 g. Sodium is elevated to reflect seasoning and broth. Values represent a best-estimate composite for the edible portion of the dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on shellfish/seafood protein-dominant dish with minimal carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Garlic Crayfish in Golden Broth. Crayfish is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous protein-dominant seafood dishes (e.g., boiled prawns, cooked shellfish) which have GI values in the range of 0–10 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Carbohydrate content per 100 g is approximately 3.8 g (primarily from garlic and broth thickeners), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 1.0 per 500 g serving (edible yield ~200 g). GI category is low (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seasoned broth and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (edible portion), which can contribute substantially to daily sodium limits of 2000 mg when consumed in typical restaurant serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Garlic Crayfish in Golden Broth is a low-carbohydrate, high-protein dish with an estimated GI of 5 and glycemic load of approximately 1.0 per serving, making it generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments; however, sodium content warrants monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and dietary cholesterol from crayfish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While crayfish provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health, the high sodium content of the seasoned broth and the dietary cholesterol content of approximately 118 mg per 100 g require individuals with cardiovascular disease to consume this dish in moderation and request reduced-sodium preparation where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Crayfish in Golden Broth (金汤蒜蓉小龙虾, jīn tāng suàn róng xiǎo lóng xiā) is a popular Chinese seafood dish featuring live or fresh crayfish (procambarus clarkii, commonly called 'small lobster' in Chinese culinary tradition) cooked in a richly flavoured golden broth made from chicken or pork bone stock, turmeric or egg yolk for colour, and an abundance of minced garlic. The dish originates from the crayfish cuisine tradition of Hubei and Sichuan provinces in China, where crayfish became a street-food and restaurant staple from the 1990s onwards. In Singapore, it is served in Chinese restaurants as a premium shared dish, typically priced at SGD 40–60 for a portion. The crayfish are typically blanched or stir-fried first, then simmered in the golden broth with garlic, ginger, chilli, and aromatics until the shells are bright red and the meat is tender and juicy. The flavour profile is savoury, garlicky, mildly spicy, and umami-rich, with the golden broth providing a silky, slightly sweet counterpoint to the bold garlic and chilli notes. Texturally, the crayfish meat is firm, slightly springy, and succulent. Nutritionally, the edible portion is high in protein (approximately 14.8 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (4.2 g per 100 g), and low in carbohydrates (3.8 g per 100 g), making it a low-glycaemic-index dish (estimated GI 5). Sodium content is elevated due to seasoning and broth, estimated at 480 mg per 100 g, which is a key clinical consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and zinc from the crayfish. It is not suitable for individuals with crustacean or shellfish allergies. Halal status is uncertain and depends on restaurant certification.

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked crayfish (FDC ID 174202) with composite adjustments for garlic, golden broth, and seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate based on protein-dominant seafood dishes from peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary protein source (cooked crayfish) has strong USDA SR Legacy backing, and the adjustments are methodologically defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy crayfish entry (FDC ID 174202) and is not reliably estimated for this composite dish. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy source for cooked crayfish and cannot be reliably estimated for the composite dish. 3. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is set to 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in cooked freshwater crustacean per USDA SR Legacy — confirmed zero for the crayfish component; trace amounts from cooking oil are below detection threshold. 4. Monounsaturated fat (1.4 g) and polyunsaturated fat (1.1 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked crayfish fatty acid profile. 5. Sodium is elevated above raw crayfish values to reflect seasoning, soy sauce, and broth additions typical of restaurant preparation. 6. Halal status is marked false as restaurant halal certification is not confirmed from the field survey listing. 7. GI is estimated at 5 (low) based on analogous protein-dominant seafood dishes; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 8. The dish is sold at SGD 51.80 at Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant (万家福创新中国菜), Circular Road, Singapore, per field survey data. 9. Cuisine tag 'Roasted Duck' in the input appears to be a field survey platform restaurant category tag and does not describe this dish; it has been disregarded for food classification purposes.
